summary: DeVault argues that white-collar employees have far more leverage over their employers than they realize, and should strategically exercise that power. He reframes the employment relationship as one between equals pursuing mutual goals, where your real job is simply to make money for the business. The post provides tactical advice on exercising agency unilaterally, building team solidarity, using strategic subversion, and weaponizing HR processes. He covers goals ranging from small (choosing your own tools, skipping meetings) to large (negotiating a four-day workweek, replacing a manager). Throughout, he emphasizes that employers already use tactics, propaganda, and subterfuge, so employees should feel no qualms about doing the same.
key_points: Your employer profits at least 1.5x your salary from your work — you matter to them more than they let on, and basic financial arithmetic proves your leverage
Your real job is to make money for the business, not to follow your job description — any change that serves that goal is defensible
Exercise agency by acting first and asking permission never: 'I'll be working from home Friday' beats 'can I work from home Friday?'
HR exists to protect the company from you, not to help you — but understanding the risks they manage (lawsuits, unsafe conditions) lets you press the right buttons
Team solidarity is your strongest lever; if your team collectively agrees to work on its own terms while still delivering results, no one will protest
money_quotes: A healthy employment relationship between an employee and employer is that of two entities who agree to work together on equal terms to strive towards mutual goals.
Remember your real job – to make money – and you can adjust the parameters of your working environment relatively freely provided that you are still aligned with this goal.
You need not only to make money, but to be seen making money.
Ranked in descending order of leverage within the business: your team, your boss, you.
Don't be above a little bit of subversion and deception; your employer is using those tools against you too.
HR is the enemy; their job is to protect the company from you.
HR should either view you as mostly harmless or look upon you with fear, but nothing in between.
Don't forget to actually write some code or whatever when you're not busy planning a corporate coup.
key_insight: Employees who understand their financial value to the business and strategically exercise agency — through unilateral action, team solidarity, and calculated use of corporate systems — can reshape their working conditions far more than the typical power dynamic suggests.
